summary: Stancil argues that most tech work is unglamorous grunt work and that the belief we’re just one reorg or tool upgrade away from “strategic” work is mostly a comforting illusion. He suggests the mundane tasks are often the real value, and AI is likely to shift the tedium rather than eliminate it, creating new layers of coordination and review.
money_quotes: The argument for the second point is that it is empirically true.
There are only so much strategy to discuss, and so many banner projects to be done, and most of what most companies need is tedium and organizational caulk.
But does having the former—robots to do rote work—necessarily imply the latter—that our jobs will be better?
No matter how many things technology makes convenient, the supply of inconvenient things seems to remain constant.
key_insight: The promise that better tools or AI will free knowledge workers for “strategic” work is overstated; in practice, technology tends to rearrange and multiply the tedium rather than remove it.
